content.text: Certificate-Based Authentication (CBA) in Entra ID allows users to authenticate using an X.509 digital certificate instead of a password. The private key associated with the certificate is stored on the user's device (software certificate store), a hardware smart card (PIV or CAC), or a hardware security module. The authentication proof is a cryptographic signature over a challenge — the private key never leaves the authenticator. This is phishing-resistant by definition.

Entra CBA was introduced to support US government FedRAMP and CMMC requirements, where smart card authentication (PIV/CAC) is mandated. It is also used by enterprises that already maintain an Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS) PKI and want to leverage it for Entra ID authentication without migrating to a different credential type.

attrs.alt: Entra ID CBA architecture — PKI trust chain, configuration, and authentication flow

content.text: If your organisation already has a PKI (Active Directory Certificate Services), CBA is often the lowest-friction path to phishing-resistant MFA — no new hardware to procure, no new CA to stand up.

Before any user can authenticate with CBA, Entra ID must trust the CA that issued their certificate. Upload the Root CA and all Intermediate/Issuing CA certificates to the tenant's certificateBasedAuthConfiguration, along with the CRL Distribution Point URL that Entra ID will use to check revocation.

# Configure trusted Certificate Authorities for CBA in Entra ID
# Requires: Authentication Policy Administrator or Global Administrator

import requests, base64

TENANT_ID = 'YOUR_TENANT_ID'

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
        data={
            'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
            'client_id': 'YOUR_APP_ID',
            'client_secret': 'YOUR_SECRET',
            'scope': 'https://graph.microsoft.com/.default',
        }
    )
    return r.json()['access_token']

def upload_trusted_ca(token, cert_der_bytes, is_root_ca=True, crl_url=None, ocsp_url=None):
    headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}', 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}

    # cert_der_bytes: DER-encoded CA certificate bytes
    cert_b64 = base64.b64encode(cert_der_bytes).decode()

    # Read the current trusted CA list
    resp = requests.get(
        'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization',
        headers=headers
    )
    org_id = resp.json()['value'][0]['id']

    # Get current certificateBasedAuthConfiguration
    current = requests.get(
        f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization/{org_id}/certificateBasedAuthConfiguration',
        headers=headers
    ).json().get('value', [{}])[0]

    existing_cas = current.get('certificateAuthorities', [])

    new_ca = {
        'isRootAuthority': is_root_ca,
        'certificate': cert_b64,
    }
    if crl_url:
        new_ca['certificateRevocationListUrl'] = crl_url
    if ocsp_url:
        new_ca['deltaCertificateRevocationListUrl'] = ocsp_url

    existing_cas.append(new_ca)

    config_id = current.get('id')
    if config_id:
        # Update existing config
        resp = requests.patch(
            f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization/{org_id}'
            f'/certificateBasedAuthConfiguration/{config_id}',
            headers=headers,
            json={'certificateAuthorities': existing_cas}
        )
    else:
        # Create new config
        resp = requests.post(
            f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization/{org_id}/certificateBasedAuthConfiguration',
            headers=headers,
            json={'certificateAuthorities': existing_cas}
        )
    print(f'CA uploaded: {resp.status_code}')

# Example: read a DER cert file and upload
with open('/path/to/issuing-ca.der', 'rb') as f:
    ca_bytes = f.read()

token = get_token()
upload_trusted_ca(
    token,
    ca_bytes,
    is_root_ca=False,   # False = issuing/intermediate CA
    crl_url='http://crl.contoso.com/contoso-issuing.crl',
    ocsp_url='http://ocsp.contoso.com'
)
Username Binding and Authentication Strength Rules

Username binding tells Entra ID which field in the certificate holds the user's identity. The most common binding is Subject Alternative Name (SAN) RFC822 → userPrincipalName. Authentication strength rules allow different certificates (smart card vs software cert) to satisfy different MFA levels.

# Configure username binding rules for CBA
# Username binding maps a certificate field to an Entra ID user attribute
# This tells Entra ID which user the certificate belongs to

import requests

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        'https://login.microsoftonline.com/YOUR_TENANT_ID/oauth2/v2.0/token',
        data={
            'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
            'client_id': 'YOUR_APP_ID',
            'client_secret': 'YOUR_SECRET',
            'scope': 'https://graph.microsoft.com/.default',
        }
    )
    return r.json()['access_token']

token = get_token()
headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}', 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}

# Read current CBA policy
resp = requests.get(
    'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationMethodsPolicy'
    '/authenticationMethodConfigurations/X509Certificate',
    headers=headers
)
print(resp.json())

# Configure CBA with username binding rules
cba_policy = {
    'state': 'enabled',
    'includeTargets': [{'targetType': 'group', 'id': 'all_users'}],
    'authenticationModeConfiguration': {
        'x509CertificateAuthenticationDefaultMode': 'x509CertificateMultiFactor',
        # 'x509CertificateSingleFactor' or 'x509CertificateMultiFactor'
        # MultiFactor: cert acts as both factors (phishing-resistant MFA)
        # SingleFactor: cert = first factor, second factor required separately
        'rules': [
            {
                # High-assurance certificate (e.g. smart card with PIN) -> MFA
                'x509CertificateRuleType': 'issuerSubject',
                'identifier': 'CN=Contoso Smart Card CA',
                'x509CertificateAuthenticationMode': 'x509CertificateMultiFactor',
            },
            {
                # Software cert (e.g. from Autopilot provisioning) -> single factor
                'x509CertificateRuleType': 'issuerSubject',
                'identifier': 'CN=Contoso Software CA',
                'x509CertificateAuthenticationMode': 'x509CertificateSingleFactor',
            }
        ]
    },
    'x509CertificateUserBindings': [
        {
            # Primary binding: SAN RFC822 email address -> userPrincipalName
            'x509CertificateField': 'subjectAlternativeName',
            'odataType': '#microsoft.graph.x509CertificateUserBinding',
            'userProperty': 'userPrincipalName',
            'priority': 1
        },
        {
            # Fallback: Subject DN CN -> displayName (for legacy certs)
            'x509CertificateField': 'subjectCN',
            'odataType': '#microsoft.graph.x509CertificateUserBinding',
            'userProperty': 'onPremisesUserPrincipalName',
            'priority': 2
        }
    ]
}

resp = requests.patch(
    'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationMethodsPolicy'
    '/authenticationMethodConfigurations/X509Certificate',
    headers=headers, json=cba_policy
)
print(f'CBA policy updated: {resp.status_code}')
Most common — email address in cert SAN

PIV (Personal Identity Verification) and CAC (Common Access Card) are US government-standard smart cards that carry an X.509 certificate in a hardware chip. CBA in Entra ID supports these cards natively — the user inserts the card, enters their PIN (unlocking the card's private key), and authenticates without a password.

# Smart card (PIV/CAC) sign-in with Entra ID CBA
# PIV = Personal Identity Verification (US government standard)
# CAC = Common Access Card (US military)
# Both use X.509 certificates on hardware smart cards

# Prerequisites for smart card CBA:
# 1. Smart card reader attached to workstation
# 2. Card middleware installed (ActivClient, OpenSC, etc.)
# 3. Issuing CA certificate uploaded to Entra ID CBA trusted CAs
# 4. Username binding configured (SAN or Subject)
# 5. CBA enabled in Authentication Methods Policy

# The sign-in experience:
# User navigates to portal.azure.com or any Entra ID app
# At the sign-in page: click 'Sign in with certificate'
# Windows prompts for smart card PIN
# Entra ID receives the certificate, validates the chain, checks revocation
# Maps cert to user via username binding
# Issues token (no password, no push notification)

# Verify a user's certificate is valid for CBA (PowerShell)
# Run on a machine with the smart card inserted

$cert = Get-Item Cert:\CurrentUser\My | Where-Object {
    $_.Subject -match 'contoso' -and $_.NotAfter -gt (Get-Date)
}

if (-not $cert) { Write-Warning 'No valid client certificate found'; return }

Write-Output 'Certificate found:'
Write-Output "  Subject:    $($cert.Subject)"
Write-Output "  Issuer:     $($cert.Issuer)"
Write-Output "  SAN:        $(($cert.Extensions | Where-Object Oid.FriendlyName -eq 'Subject Alternative Name').Format($false))"
Write-Output "  Thumbprint: $($cert.Thumbprint)"
Write-Output "  Expires:    $($cert.NotAfter)"

# Verify the certificate chain
$chain = New-Object System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Chain
$chain.Build($cert)
foreach ($element in $chain.ChainElements) {
    Write-Output "  Chain: $($element.Certificate.Subject)"
}
Write-Output "  Chain valid: $($chain.ChainStatus.Count -eq 0)"

When a user leaves the organisation or a certificate is compromised, the CA revokes the certificate by adding it to the Certificate Revocation List (CRL). Entra ID checks the CRL or OCSP responder at every CBA sign-in. The CDP URL embedded in the certificate must be publicly accessible from the internet — internal URLs will fail.

# Certificate revocation — CRL and OCSP configuration
# Entra ID checks revocation at every CBA sign-in
# The CRL Distribution Point (CDP) URL is embedded in the certificate

import requests, base64
from cryptography import x509
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend

def inspect_certificate_cdp(cert_pem_path):
    with open(cert_pem_path, 'rb') as f:
        cert = x509.load_pem_x509_certificate(f.read(), default_backend())

    print(f'Subject: {cert.subject}')
    print(f'Issuer:  {cert.issuer}')
    print(f'Valid:   {cert.not_valid_before_utc} to {cert.not_valid_after_utc}')
    print(f'Serial:  {hex(cert.serial_number)}')

    # Extract CDP (CRL Distribution Point) URLs
    try:
        cdp_ext = cert.extensions.get_extension_for_class(x509.CRLDistributionPoints)
        print('CRL Distribution Points:')
        for dp in cdp_ext.value:
            for name in dp.full_name:
                print(f'  {name.value}')
    except x509.ExtensionNotFound:
        print('No CRL Distribution Points (Entra ID cannot check revocation!)')

    # Extract OCSP URL
    try:
        aia_ext = cert.extensions.get_extension_for_class(x509.AuthorityInformationAccess)
        for desc in aia_ext.value:
            if desc.access_method == x509.oid.AuthorityInformationAccessOID.OCSP:
                print(f'OCSP URL: {desc.access_location.value}')
    except x509.ExtensionNotFound:
        print('No OCSP URL')

    return cert

# Ensure the CDP URL is reachable from Entra ID's perspective
# Entra ID must be able to fetch the CRL or OCSP response over the internet
# CDP/OCSP URLs must be publicly accessible (not behind corporate firewall)
# Internal CDP URLs (http://pki.contoso.com/...) WILL FAIL for Entra CBA
# Solution: publish CRL to Azure Blob Storage or an internet-facing web server

def check_crl_reachable(crl_url):
    try:
        resp = requests.get(crl_url, timeout=10)
        print(f'CRL reachable: {resp.status_code} ({len(resp.content)} bytes)')
        crl = x509.load_der_x509_crl(resp.content, default_backend())
        print(f'CRL next update: {crl.next_update_utc}')
        print(f'Revoked certs:   {len(list(crl))}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'CRL not reachable: {e}')

check_crl_reachable('http://crl.contoso.com/contoso-issuing.crl')
CRL URLs must be internet-accessible — publish to Azure Blob Storage or a public CDN endpoint
CRL lifetime should be short (hours to 1 day) to minimise the window between revocation and enforcement
OCSP provides real-time revocation status and is preferred over CRL for time-sensitive revocation
If the CRL URL is unreachable at sign-in time, Entra ID fails the authentication by default (fail-closed)

CBA sign-ins are recorded in Entra ID sign-in logs with authenticationMethod = 'X.509 certificate'. Monitoring for failed CBA sign-ins surfaces revocation failures, expired certificates, and misconfigured username bindings.

# Monitor CBA sign-in events and certificate details
import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        'https://login.microsoftonline.com/YOUR_TENANT_ID/oauth2/v2.0/token',
        data={
            'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
            'client_id': 'YOUR_APP_ID',
            'client_secret': 'YOUR_SECRET',
            'scope': 'https://graph.microsoft.com/.default',
        }
    )
    return r.json()['access_token']

def audit_cba_signins(token, days=7):
    headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'}
    since = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(days=days)).strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')

    # Sign-ins where x509Certificate was used
    resp = requests.get(
        'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns'
        f'?$filter=createdDateTime ge {since}'
        ' and authenticationDetails/any(d: d/authenticationMethod eq \'X.509 certificate\')'
        '&$select=userPrincipalName,createdDateTime,status,authenticationDetails'
        ',conditionalAccessStatus&$top=200',
        headers={**headers, 'ConsistencyLevel': 'eventual'}
    )
    events = resp.json().get('value', [])

    success = [e for e in events if e.get('status', {}).get('errorCode') == 0]
    failed  = [e for e in events if e.get('status', {}).get('errorCode') != 0]

    print(f'CBA sign-ins (last {days} days):')
    print(f'  Successful: {len(success)}')
    print(f'  Failed:     {len(failed)}')

    if failed:
        print('\nFailed CBA sign-ins (first 5):')
        for e in failed[:5]:
            code = e.get('status', {}).get('errorCode')
            msg  = e.get('status', {}).get('failureReason', '')
            print(f'  {e["createdDateTime"][:19]}  {e["userPrincipalName"]}  error={code}: {msg[:60]}')

token = get_token()
audit_cba_signins(token, days=7)

CBA depends on certificates issued by your PKI. Expired or revoked certificates cause authentication failures, so proactive lifecycle management is critical. Unlike FIDO2 keys or Windows Hello, CBA certificates have a fixed validity period (typically 1–3 years) that must be tracked and renewed before expiry.

Use your CA's built-in reporting to enumerate expiring certificates, then notify affected users and trigger renewal workflows. The example below queries AD CS and sends email reminders via Microsoft Graph.

# Find certificates expiring within 30 days
# Entra ID doesn't manage cert expiry natively — use PKI CA CRL and Graph sign-ins

# PowerShell: Query AD CS for expiring certs (run on CA server)
certutil -view -restrict "NotAfter>=NOW,NotAfter<=(NOW+30:00:00:00),Disposition=20" \
         -out "RequesterName,NotBefore,NotAfter,SerialNumber,SubjectKeyIdentifier" csv

# Alternative: Check user cert in Entra sign-in logs to detect stale certs
$signinFilter = '?$filter=authenticationMethodsUsed/any(m: m eq ' + "'certificateBasedAuthentication'" + ')' +
                '&$select=userPrincipalName,createdDateTime,status,authenticationMethodsUsed'
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns$signinFilter" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Where-Object { $_.status.errorCode -eq 500121 }  # AADSTS500121 = cert auth required but failed

# PowerShell: Notify users with certs expiring soon via Microsoft Graph Mail
$expiringUsers = @("alice@contoso.com", "bob@contoso.com")  # populate from your PKI
foreach ($upn in $expiringUsers) {
    $body = @{
        message = @{
            subject = "Your smart card certificate expires in 30 days"
            body    = @{ contentType = "Text"; content = "Please request a renewal from IT." }
            toRecipients = @(@{ emailAddress = @{ address = $upn } })
        }
        saveToSentItems = $false
    }
    Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
        -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/notifications@contoso.com/sendMail" \
        -Body ($body | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5)
}
Set certificate validity to 2 years maximum — shorter periods reduce exposure from lost cards
Enable auto-enrollment via Group Policy (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Cryptography\AutoEnrollment\AEPolicy) for domain-joined devices
For smart cards, configure the Card Management System (CMS) to automatically renew 30 days before expiry
After renewal, the old certificate remains valid until its NotAfter date — revoke it if the card was replaced
Monitor AADSTS error codes in sign-in logs: 500121 (cert required, not provided), 500123 (cert expired), 500124 (cert revoked)
Migrating from AD FS Certificate Authentication to Entra CBA

Organizations that federated with Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS) for smart card authentication can migrate to Entra native CBA, eliminating the AD FS infrastructure dependency. Entra CBA became generally available in 2022 and achieves feature parity with AD FS certificate authentication for most scenarios.

The migration is non-destructive if done in stages: enable Entra CBA in parallel with AD FS, test with a pilot group, then convert the domain from federated to managed authentication. After conversion, AD FS is no longer in the authentication path.

# Step 1: Verify Entra CBA is configured and tested in audit mode
# Set authenticationStrength to require CBA for a pilot group
$params = @{
    displayName = "CBA Pilot — Smart Card Users"
    conditions = @{
        users = @{ includeGroups = @("<pilot-group-object-id>") }
    }
    grantControls = @{
        operator = "OR"
        authenticationStrength = @{ id = "<your-cba-strength-policy-id>" }
    }
}
New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -BodyParameter $params

# Step 2: Confirm users can authenticate via Entra CBA before cutting over
# Check sign-ins for the pilot group:
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET -Uri (
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns" +
    "?$filter=authenticationMethodsUsed/any(m: m eq 'certificateBasedAuthentication')" +
    "&$top=50") | Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

# Step 3: Convert federated domain to managed (cuts off AD FS)
# WARNING: Run this only after validating Entra CBA for ALL affected users
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Domain.ReadWrite.All"
$domain = Get-MgDomain -DomainId "contoso.com"
# Update authentication type from Federated to Managed
Update-MgDomainFederationConfiguration -DomainId "contoso.com" -SigningCertificate $null
# Or use Set-MsolDomainAuthentication if using MSOL:
# Set-MsolDomainAuthentication -DomainName contoso.com -Authentication Managed

# Step 4: Validate post-cutover — CBA sign-ins should now appear in Entra logs
# AD FS sign-in logs will go dark for CBA users after conversion
UPN from SAN or custom claim rule
UPN, Email, SKI, SHA1 via binding policy
Prerequisite: All users' UPNs in Entra ID must match what is embedded in their certificates (SAN or subject CN). Run a pre-migration audit to find mismatches before converting the domain.

Certificate-Based Authentication enables phishing-resistant authentication using X.509 certificates — including PIV/CAC smart cards for US government requirements. Entra ID validates the certificate chain against trusted CAs, checks revocation via CRL or OCSP, maps the certificate to a user via configurable username bindings, and issues a token — no password or MFA push involved. For organisations with an existing PKI, CBA is often the fastest path to phishing-resistant MFA with no new hardware investment.
Upload Root CA and Issuing CA certificates with publicly reachable CRL URLs to enable CBA
Configure username binding rules to map certificate SAN or Subject fields to Entra ID user attributes
Set authentication strength to x509CertificateMultiFactor for smart cards (satisfies phishing-resistant MFA tier)
CRL Distribution Point URLs must be internet-accessible — internal URLs cause sign-in failures
CBA satisfies FedRAMP High and NIST AAL3 requirements when combined with hardware-backed smart cards
Monitor failed CBA sign-ins for revocation errors, expired certificates, and binding mismatches
